If I had to, if I only had one chance to make something work, I'd use a story. First person story, the third person story. Either way, it's a story. If I only had one chance to make something work. The story's not reach certain kinds of people. Yeah, they're not going to work for everybody and they're going to work better for different, coming from a certain format, they're going to work better for different products than others. And in different markets than others. This is a stupac promotion for Vegas World. And this is first person direct pitch. He's got, I started the counter last night and I got distracted by a telephone conference, but I think he's got maybe 60 of the 97 things in this book. Give this letter. I mean this guy is just fabulous. He did all the, yeah, Bob Wright's all his own kind. And you should get out his mailing list and get his stuff and really study it because this guy is just phenomenal. You know, we're going to talk about the word pictures later. He does a beautiful job with that. He should be selling propositions to superiors, more or more on the superb. In fact, we're the word pictures now aren't we? Yeah. Listen to this word picture. You get, he's pitched you're going to get a free camera. You get your camera when you check in. Load it up with film so you can take my picture when I'm walking through the lobby or ask me to take yours. Or maybe you'll take a snapshot of one of the stars of our galaxy showroom heading for our high payout video poker machines. You'll use your camera again for a second location because I'm throwing in the most desirable resort locations in the world. For reported to the Bahamas, Puerto Vallarta, and Mexico, Orlando, home of Disney World, Scottsdale, Arizona, the Gulf of the 7th and rich and famous Kauai, Hawaii. Now what he's done is he's got you using the camera. That's the brilliance of that. What's that got? Free. Yeah. And I mean he's put you in the picture and got you using it. And seeing things through it. Yeah. Oh, I love that. I mean it's brilliant. The main head, by the way, the main head is taken. And you receive absolutely free of beautiful cameras. And they pitch the camera. They pitch the camera. Exactly. Exactly. But he's got to use it and have it follow the location. The top, the handwritten headline at the top is take the picture of a guy who's crazy enough to give you the camera to do it with. Crush your choice of five locations. That's a word picture. And what a word picture is all about is putting that person into the picture. And try to have them experience what that's like. As opposed to a word picture, like I know with his offer you get a bunch of chips and this and that and the other. As opposed to a word picture of this whole mountain of free things you get. Yeah. Well, a word picture, like if you want to talk about the chips and the money they get, you would describe them. You know, and now you're sitting down at the blackjack table, but instead of taking your money out of your pocket, you're taking the chips I gave you for free. And you're getting real money back. And so the person is at the table playing. That's a word picture. So there's an action element. Yeah. You know, instead of a company, instead of describing Hawaii, like most people would describe Hawaii, now you and your wife are on the beach at sunset taking a stroll and holding hand and you paint the... And the next thing is involvement devices. Involvement devices are quizzes, ask yourself these five questions. If you answer yes to any three of the above five questions, that kind of stuff. How to use... To determine whether or not it's worth your while to pay close and serious attention to everything I have to say. Take a look at the ten items listed below. How many are of great interest to you? Look at these ten and see how many are of great interest to you. Sometimes I would write that as, take a highlighter pen and mark through the ones that are a break. Check off the one circle the numbers of... Of course the biggest users of involvement devices are publishers, clearing house, readers, you know, the guys that use sweepstakes and stamps and put this circle here and tear this there. I don't use that stuff much and most of my clients don't because it's too expensive in small quantity mailings to do all that shit. But this is the next closest thing to it, is getting them to take a little test or fill this out or... You'll find that Good Direct Response Copy often asks the reader questions all the way through the copy. Every so often it will turn from declarative to a question designed really to get a yes. Does that sound... Yes, it's a trick that you need to do to get a yes. Yeah, wouldn't you agree? There's a copy I just wrote, I didn't bring it, I just wrote it for Larry Pino. The copy goes in selling a business training type move to you. And there's a whole bunch of copy drawing the analogy to starting a business to standing looking out over a minefield and knowing that there's mines planted all the way through the dirt. You don't know where they are and if you step on anyone up and it blows up and your light comes off and blood, gore, you know, etc. Then the copy says, but imagine if you were standing there looking out over the minefield with X-rayed glasses on and you could see where all the mines were and could see the best and shortest path to the other side, would that make a difference to you? The guy has to say, sure, that would make a big difference to me. Yeah, I like that. And again, it's selling because if we're in person to person selling, one of the things we're taught to do is build a momentum of yes responses to questions. Trial closes all the way through the selling process. When I started direct selling, you know, I mean, it was when you started closing with the first word out of your mouth, you know, what do you do? You ask questions to build a yes momentum during the sales presentation so they're used to saying yes to you while we're doing the same thing in print. The last thing we'll talk about, and I think we'll take a break, is internal repetition. In the wrong form, again, in personal selling, the typical sales presentation is actually like three because a presentation will be made, the customer will raise two or three objections. The sales person now is going to go back and answer the objections and in the course of so doing, he just about does his whole presentation over again. Usually there's another set of objections. He does it again and finally he closes the sale. Another analogy I'll draw is in business to business marketing and business to business selling, most sales are closed after the third or the seventh call, not on the first call. So now knowing those two things, we know it's going to take repetition in terms of sequence to close the sale. We also know that we should have some repetition within the presentation itself. So I'll try and say the same with things three or more times within the context of the ad or the letter, said slightly differently, but delivering exactly the same benefit argument, repetitively. For those who read everything, I get the value of the repetition. For those who skim and come in at some point along the way, I have a chance of getting them because I'm repeating. If you go back and get the old Joe Carvo ad, as I used to diagram this in seminars, you will find a complete pitch repeated six times in the ad. From the, from the, I was poor and now I'm rich right through to buy the book. You'll find the complete pitch repeated six times. I'm just saying business is four to seven. Sure. That's when they also come. Yeah. Is that television? What's happened with infomercials because we know people go in and out and they started different places and they see pieces over here. Most people like leaving, it makes discoveries to tell you he builds three, seven and a half minute shows. And then those are hooked together with commercials. It's 28 and a half minutes long. So if there's going to be like he and I are working on a show with Crescent right now. So if there's going to be, here's what's going to be in the show. There's going to be a group of people taken off the street who are going to over over a period of a day, learn mental powers like Crescent has. So there's going to be no over two, two weekends, excuse me. So they're going to come to the seminar. They're going to have a week and they're going to come back to the seminar. So there's people going to come off the street. They're going to come in the room for the seminar. Then they're going to go about their business for a week and come back and tell what has happened to them with their improved mental powers. That's one thing that's going to happen. Second thing that's going to happen in the show is of course Crescent is going to do some kind of dramatic demonstration. As he always does. The third thing that's going to happen in the show is there's going to be testimonies. Both famous people and real people. The fourth thing that's going to happen in the show is Crescent is going to teach Mike how to do something and Mike's going to be able to immediately do it. So those are the things that are going to happen in the show. Every one of those things is going to happen in every one of the three segments. So we're going to see a different person who was in the seminar and went out into their real world and came back and said we found our lost dog. We found my mother's brooch that's been lost for 20 years. Money fell out of the sky and hit me, whatever. There's going to be a different one of those in each of the three segments. There's going to be a different demonstration in each of the three segments. There's going to be a different, I teach Mike, Mike does it back to me in each of the three segments. So we're doing a whole show three times. Now each of those will be different, but structurally it's the same. I'll do the same thing in the long form sales line. I'll go through the entire situation and then I'll go through it again and then I'll go through it again. And twist it just enough so the reader doesn't quickly identify what the hell I'm doing. People don't see that. No. They're mostly wrong. Yeah. You need a long letter to do that. You sure do. You sure do. No. No. No. No. So I'm going to give you an example of this. This one. I tell them, in the very first paragraph, they're called South Seas, I don't know how you pronounce this, Moby, I guess, Pearls. They're large 15 millimeter grade A plus rated by the jewelry industry. And I'm going to tell them that same thing somewhere. Where is it? Maybe all the way down in the PS. It's all the way at the end of the letter. PS, I have to emphasize this is South World Jewelry, which I told them earlier. And large 15 millimeter grade A plus Moby Pearls from the South Seas. A plus is the highest, best possible rating for natural pearls. There is nothing better or more valuable. You could literally search the entire globe, find pearl fields, hire divers, go from the Orient to Australia and back to the South Seas and still not find anything better than the pearl that's going to be in your pendant lawyer. Then, by the way, this is a side issue. We've been talking to people and it's what occurred to me first of all. When I think of pearls, I think of them coming from China, where you think of Japan. By the way, I guess a lot of people think about pearls coming from Japan or China, and a lot do, but nothing beats the brilliance, lust or beauty and flawless perfection of these exotic South Seas pearls. And so that repetition is there throughout the county. You guys want to break for lunch? Yes, I've got a lot of time to go to all this. Okay, pick up what we left off. Features versus benefits, I would hope to God, I don't need to teach. But you would be amazed at how much stuff you pick up. And you see people spending good money on direct mount campaigns and advertising, and still what you got is a list of features, and they have not turned them into benefits. My rule, 35, is never leave anything to be translated by the reader. You say to yourself, well, they're obviously going to know what the benefit of this is, but tell them, don't leave it up to them to figure that out. 36 is use every benefit you got. There's no such thing as too many bullet points. Boardroom reports stuff is maybe the best example out there of pure bullet point advertising and direct response. I mean, they're quite capable of having 500 bullet points for a given book. And they do very, very, very, very well. So you need to drain yourself when you've got a product or an offer of every possible benefit, and list every one, because different things hit different people at different times. 37 is a real interesting approach to copy. It should be used more often than it is, because it always works. And that is positioning yourself as the intended customer's ally and advocate against a mutual enemy. Ted Nicholas has made fortune with lawyers as the enemy and the IRS as the enemy. I mean, the entire How to Form Your Own Corporation without a lawyer for under $75 book, $200 million worth of that book, $200 million worth of that book of its own. The book's How to Form Your Own Corporation without a lawyer for under $75. And all of the advertising is, us small business guys against these nasty, bastard lawyers who charge a fortune and have their secretaries do all the work, and that pitches just fail safe. Jeff, with his financial planners and insurance guys, the Home Office is the enemy. All the idiots at the Home Office who won't let you do what you need to do to make money and don't understand what you guys do in the field, and take three-hour lunches and play around with their secretaries while you're out chasing down an appointment in the Interlands. And by getting on their side against the Home Office, whichever life insurance guy on earth hates the Home Office, so you immediately position yourself on the right side of the fence. And this is just a great strategy. Secrets. It's my contention that you have to let people off the hook for the situation they're in in order to sell them your solution. You can't make it their fault. And you have to let people off the hook. You cannot make it their fault that they're fat, or that they're dumb, or that they're broke, or that they're... Susan Paulder brilliantly let them all off the hook. It's not your fault. The diet industry screwed you. The diet industry has lied to you, the diet industry has abused you. It's not your fault. And if you don't do that, you can't get them on your side. Well, one of the ways that you can always do that is, and I believe people devoutly believe it, is that there is some secret that has been conspiratorially withheld from them, that if they just knew that, they'd be in the after picture instead of in the before picture. And so, you know, the reason you can't pay your bills, and the reason that you're one step away from having your cars repossessed, and the reason you're not doing well financially, is because the sunbitch, few rich guys and bankers have all got together, and they haven't told you this one missing link. If you just knew this, but they've kept it from you. People want to believe that there are these secrets out there, that if they just knew them, everything would be dramatically different in their lives. There's a travel promotion now that it's a knockoff of a board of reports promotion. I forget who's running it, but I've been told they're pulling tremendous numbers. And their pitch, it's for a book and a newsletter package, you know, for travelers. But their pitch is, I forget the number, but it's like the 17 secrets the airline industry does not want you to know. Five ways that people always fly first class and only pay coach. Four of this and three of that, and it totals up to X number of secrets, you know, that the airline industry lets only their buddies and their insider friends. All the pilots buddies know about this, and all the airline executives buddies know about this, and all the flight attendants' boyfriends know about this, but you don't know about this. And it's just, it's a real compelling pitch. People want to believe in secrets, and so you need a secret. This is the end of side one. Please turn the cassette over for side two.
